refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported in real-time to the Site Manager and Equipment Operator.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit measurement, reporting, and stakeholder visibility to transform a vague 'support achieving' into a verifiable, monitored outcome. The p95 percentile metric and duty cycle context are critical for acceptance criteria validation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials under representative duty cycle conditions; statistical analysis of cycle time distribution across minimum 100 representative cycles; real-time telemetry capture and post-analysis; comparison against CT_target baseline with documented site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance, operator skill level).
sub_frs: FR-CT-001: Measure and timestamp each phase of the load→travel→dump cycle with ±0.1 second accuracy.
FR-CT-002: Aggregate cycle time data and compute p95 percentile in real-time.
FR-CT-003: Transmit cycle time KPI and performance alerts to Site Manager and Equipment Operator dashboards.
FR-CT-004: Optimize vehicle propulsion, steering, and dump actuation to minimize cycle time without violating safety constraints.
FR-CT-005: Provide operator feedback (visual/auditory) on cycle time performance relative to target.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Integrate high-performance propulsion (electric or hybrid) with optimized transmission and steering control; add real-time cycle time instrumentation (sensors, timers, telemetry) and cloud-based analytics to compute and report p95 metrics.

DP-B: Deploy tele-operated vehicle with remote operator control to minimize human reaction time; instrument load/travel/dump phases with proximity and load sensors; stream telemetry to remote operator station for real-time cycle time feedback and optimization coaching.

DP-C: Implement autonomous cycle execution (load detection → travel path planning → dump trigger) with embedded cycle time optimization logic; use onboard sensors and edge computing to minimize latency; report aggregated KPI via telemetry interface.

DP-D: Hybrid approach: autonomous cycle execution with tele-operator override capability; real-time cycle time instrumentation and feedback to both autonomous controller and remote operator; cloud-based p95 analytics and performance trending.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid autonomous + tele-operated cycle execution with real-time instrumentation, edge-based cycle time optimization, and cloud analytics for p95 KPI reporting. This DP balances productivity (autonomous baseline), safety (operator override), and performance visibility (instrumentation + analytics).

potential_sources: Coupling between cycle time optimization logic (autonomous controller) and safety constraint enforcement (Safety Services) — must ensure optimization does not violate speed, acceleration, or dump angle limits.
Coupling between real-time telemetry streaming (Telemetry interface) and tele-operator control latency — high-frequency sensor data may saturate network link if not filtered/aggregated.
Coupling between edge-based cycle time computation and cloud analytics — dual computation may introduce inconsistency if not synchronized.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via clear interface contracts (Telemetry schema, SafetyAlert protocol, Tasking command format) and architectural separation of concerns (autonomous logic in Vehicle, safety enforcement in Safety Services, analytics in cloud backend). Recommend explicit latency budgets and data filtering strategy.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: CT_target is achievable within safety and regulatory constraints; risk: if target is unrealistic, optimization logic may be forced to violate safety rules.
Assumption: Network link (Telemetry, Tasking) has sufficient bandwidth and latency for real-time tele-operator control; risk: network congestion or latency spikes may degrade operator responsiveness.

Assumption: Autonomous cycle logic can be reliably trained/tuned for the agreed duty cycle; risk: off-duty-cycle scenarios (e.g., unusual terrain, load distribution) may cause cycle time degradation or safety violations.
Assumption: Operator override is always available and responsive; risk: if tele-operator is unavailable or distracted, autonomous mode must safely degrade.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) cycle time optimization constraints (e.g., max speed, max acceleration, dump angle limits derived from Safety Services); (2) network latency budget for tele-operator control (e.g., ≤200 ms round-trip); (3) fallback behavior if network link is lost or operator is unavailable; (4) data filtering/aggregation strategy for telemetry to avoid network saturation.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Autonomous and tele-operated cycle execution engine, including propulsion control, steering, dump actuation, onboard sensors (load, proximity, IMU), edge-based cycle time computation, and local safety constraint enforcement. Responsible for real-time cycle phase detection, timing, and optimization within safety envelope.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle streams cycle time measurements (phase timestamps, p95 aggregate), sensor data (load mass, position, speed), and performance metrics to cloud backend and operator dashboards. Frequency: 1–10 Hz depending on phase; payload: cycle phase, timestamp, sensor readings, computed cycle time.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives cycle commands (load, travel, dump) and optimization parameters (target speed, dump angle) from autonomous controller or tele-operator. Frequency: event-driven or periodic (e.g., per-cycle or per-phase); payload: cycle phase, target parameters, override flags.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints (speed limits, acceleration limits, dump angle limits) from Safety Services and reports safety-critical events (e.g., load instability, proximity hazard, operator override) back to Safety Services. Frequency: on-demand or periodic; payload: constraint updates, event alerts, acknowledgments.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle communicates with Teleoperation module (tele-operator commands) and cloud backend (telemetry, analytics). Latency and bandwidth constraints apply; Vehicle must handle network loss gracefully.

module_containment_scores.notes: Vehicle is the natural home for cycle execution, propulsion, steering, dump actuation, onboard sensors, and edge-based cycle time computation. Cycle time optimization logic is tightly coupled to vehicle dynamics and control; containment is high. Minor diffusion: telemetry streaming and network communication are shared with Teleoperation and cloud backend, but Vehicle owns the data generation and local computation.

Teleoperation could host tele-operator control logic and remote cycle command generation, but cycle execution and real-time optimization must remain in Vehicle for latency and safety reasons. Teleoperation would own operator interface and command translation, but not cycle timing or optimization. High diffusion risk if cycle time logic is split between Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Safety Services owns constraint definition and safety event monitoring, but not cycle execution or optimization. Placing cycle time optimization in Safety Services would create tight coupling and latency issues. Safety Services should enforce constraints via SafetyAlert interface, not execute cycle logic.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container; it should not directly implement cycle execution. Cycle logic must be delegated to Vehicle module for clarity and reusability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Cycle time KPI achievement is fundamentally a Vehicle-level concern: propulsion performance, steering responsiveness, dump actuation speed, and sensor accuracy all directly impact cycle time. Real-time optimization logic must reside in Vehicle (or edge compute co-located with Vehicle) to minimize latency and ensure safety-aware control. Teleoperation and Safety Services provide inputs (operator commands, safety constraints) via well-defined interfaces, but do not own cycle execution.
containment_risks: Risk: If cycle time optimization logic is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and cloud backend, inconsistency and latency issues will degrade performance. Mitigation: Centralize optimization in Vehicle edge compute; use Telemetry and Tasking interfaces for coordination.

Risk: If Vehicle module is overloaded with too many responsibilities (propulsion, steering, dump, sensors, optimization, safety enforcement), it becomes a monolith and difficult to test/maintain. Mitigation: Decompose Vehicle into sub-modules (propulsion controller, steering controller, dump controller, sensor fusion, optimization engine) in the next decomposition level.

Risk: Network latency between Vehicle and Teleoperation may exceed operator reaction time budget, causing tele-operator to feel unresponsive. Mitigation: Define explicit latency SLA (e.g., ≤200 ms round-trip) and implement local fallback logic in Vehicle if network is degraded.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Vehicle and Teleoperation: Vehicle owns cycle execution and real-time optimization; Teleoperation owns operator interface and high-level cycle commands. Define explicit interface contracts for Tasking (cycle commands), Telemetry (cycle time data), and SafetyAlert (constraint updates). In the next decomposition level, decompose Vehicle into propulsion, steering, dump, sensor fusion, and optimization sub-modules to avoid monolithic design.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid autonomous + tele-operated cycle execution with instrumentation and analytics) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target with real-time monitoring and reporting). The DP provides mechanisms for cycle time measurement, optimization, and KPI reporting. No functional gap identified.

diffusion_risks: Cycle time optimization logic must be centralized in Vehicle module; if distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and cloud, consistency and latency will suffer.
Telemetry interface must support high-frequency sensor streaming without saturating network; recommend data filtering/aggregation in Vehicle before transmission.
SafetyAlert interface must enforce cycle time optimization constraints in real-time; Safety Services must be able to override optimization if safety is at risk.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module is the appropriate home for cycle execution and real-time optimization. Teleoperation and Safety Services provide inputs via well-defined interfaces. Cloud backend (not explicitly modeled as a module here) would own p95 analytics and historical trending. Recommend explicit sub-module decomposition of Vehicle in next level to avoid monolithic design.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time KPI is measurable via onboard sensors (load, proximity, IMU) and timestamps. p95 percentile can be computed in Vehicle edge compute or cloud backend. Real-time reporting to operator dashboards is feasible via Telemetry interface. Field trials under representative duty cycle conditions will validate achievement of CT_target.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Vehicle module is clearly responsible for cycle execution and real-time optimization. Teleoperation module is responsible for operator interface and command translation. Safety Services is responsible for constraint enforcement. Cloud backend (implied) is responsible for p95 analytics and trending. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify cycle time optimization constraints (max speed, acceleration, dump angle) derived from Safety Services.
Define explicit latency budget for tele-operator control (e.g., ≤200 ms round-trip) and network link SLA.
Clarify fallback behavior if network link is lost or tele-operator is unavailable (e.g., autonomous mode continues with conservative cycle time targets).
In next decomposition level, decompose Vehicle into propulsion, steering, dump, sensor fusion, and optimization sub-modules.
Define Telemetry data schema and filtering strategy to avoid network saturation.
Define SafetyAlert protocol for constraint updates and safety event reporting.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and the selected DP is sound, but the Vehicle module is too coarse-grained to implement directly. Cycle time KPI achievement requires decomposition of Vehicle into propulsion control, steering control, dump actuation, sensor fusion, and optimization engine sub-modules. Each sub-module has distinct responsibilities and interfaces that must be clarified before detailed design. Additionally, the interaction between autonomous optimization and tele-operator override requires explicit sub-FR decomposition to ensure safety and responsiveness.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-VEH-001: The Vehicle propulsion system shall achieve target speed and acceleration profiles within safety limits, with real-time feedback to the optimization engine.
FR-VEH-002: The Vehicle steering system shall execute planned travel paths with ±0.5 m lateral accuracy and respond to operator commands within 200 ms.
FR-VEH-003: The Vehicle dump actuation system shall raise and lower the load container at maximum safe speed, with load stability monitoring.
FR-VEH-004: The Vehicle sensor fusion system shall provide real-time estimates of load mass, position, speed, and orientation with ≤100 ms latency.
FR-VEH-005: The Vehicle optimization engine shall compute cycle time-optimal control parameters (speed, acceleration, dump angle) subject to safety constraints from Safety Services.
FR-VEH-006: The Vehicle shall detect and report cycle phase transitions (load→travel→dump) with ±0.1 second accuracy for KPI computation.
FR-VEH-007: The Vehicle shall handle tele-operator override commands with priority over autonomous optimization, with seamless mode switching.

assumptions: CT_target is achievable within safety and regulatory constraints.
Network link (Telemetry, Tasking) has sufficient bandwidth and latency for real-time tele-operator control (≤200 ms round-trip).
Autonomous cycle logic can be reliably trained/tuned for the agreed duty cycle.
Operator override is always available and responsive; if unavailable, autonomous mode safely degrades.
Onboard sensors (load, proximity, IMU) are accurate and reliable for cycle time computation.
Safety Services provides clear, real-time constraint updates (speed limits, acceleration limits, dump angle limits).
Cloud backend has sufficient compute and storage for p95 analytics and historical trending.
open_risks: Network latency or congestion may degrade tele-operator responsiveness; mitigation: define explicit latency SLA and implement local fallback logic.
Autonomous optimization logic may conflict with safety constraints; mitigation: Safety Services must have veto power over optimization via SafetyAlert interface.
Cycle time optimization may be sensitive to duty cycle variations (terrain, load distribution, operator skill); mitigation: implement adaptive optimization with feedback from field trials.
Monolithic Vehicle module may become difficult to test and maintain; mitigation: decompose into sub-modules in next level.
Data diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and cloud backend may introduce inconsistency; mitigation: define explicit interface contracts and data ownership.
evidence_needed: Field trial data: cycle time distribution (mean, p95, p99) under representative duty cycle conditions.
Network performance data: latency, bandwidth, packet loss under typical site conditions.
Sensor accuracy data: load mass estimation error, position accuracy, speed estimation error.
Safety constraint data: speed limits, acceleration limits, dump angle limits from Safety Services.
Operator feedback: subjective assessment of tele-operator responsiveness and override usability.
Autonomous optimization performance: cycle time improvement vs. baseline operator-driven cycles.
Failure mode analysis: behavior when network is lost, operator is unavailable, or sensors fail.
